challenges.prompt: A screen reader user loads this page and presses the key to jump between landmarks. What can they do that they could NOT do with a page built entirely of <div> tags?
challenges.code:
<header>
  <nav>...</nav>
</header>
<main>
  <article>Why Cold Brew Takes 12 Hours...</article>
</main>
<footer>© 2026 The Daily Brew</footer>
options: Jump straight to <main>, skipping the header and nav they have already heard
Nothing changes — screen readers treat <header> and <div> identically
See the page render faster because semantic tags load first
Automatically translate the article into another language
challenges.explanation: Semantic elements act as landmarks. A screen reader lets the user leap directly to <main> and bypass the repeated header and navigation. With div soup there are no landmarks, so they must listen to everything from the top on every page load.

challenges.prompt: This code has a bug — what's wrong?
options: The alt text is too vague — it should describe what the image shows, e.g. "A glass of iced cold brew coffee on a wooden table"
The alt attribute should be removed entirely so the browser guesses the description
The src should come after alt for screen readers to work
needs a closing tag or the alt is ignored
challenges.explanation: Alt text should describe what the image depicts, not repeat the word "image" or the file name. The test: if you read it aloud to someone with their eyes closed, would they understand the picture? (Only purely decorative images get an empty alt="".)

challenges.prompt: Complete the tag so this self-contained blog post — one that could be syndicated to another site unchanged — uses the correct semantic element.
challenges.codeTemplate:
<{{blank}}>

Why Cold Brew Takes 12 Hours

Unlike hot coffee, cold brew relies on time...
challenges.explanation: <article> is for self-contained content that would still make sense copy-pasted to another site — exactly a standalone blog post. <section> would imply it only makes sense as part of the current page.

challenges.prompt: Arrange these lines into a valid semantic page skeleton, from the top of the body to the bottom.
challenges.explanation: Semantic landmarks flow top to bottom: the <header> (holding the <nav>) comes first, then the single <main> with the page's primary content, and finally the <footer>. This mirrors how a screen reader announces the page and how the layout reads visually.
